agent1_specific: You have recently become a full partner in a firm that represents and manages high-profile figures in science, exploration, and technology. The astronaut in question, while not the firm’s most lucrative account, is your first assignment as a partner and you want to demonstrate that you can secure favorable terms. You met him for the first time yesterday. He is a seasoned astronaut in his late fifties, still physically fit and mentally sharp for his age. During your introduction meeting, you learned that while he has flown in multiple missions over his long career—including shuttle flights and orbital research operations—he has not held a primary leadership post in more than three years. Meanwhile, his public presence and perceived relevance within space exploration circles has declined somewhat as new generations of astronauts entered the spotlight. The country’s space agency, for which he has flown many times, has a major international lunar research mission scheduled for launch in three weeks. The role of Mission Commander is widely considered one of the most challenging and influential positions an astronaut can hold, due to its responsibilities for crew coordination, communications, and in-field mission execution. In past discussions with the astronaut, he emphasized how much this opportunity means to him. Although he has been active as a mentor and involved in public outreach programs, he has longed for a major return to hands-on spaceflight. Commanding the lunar mission could serve as a defining milestone late in his career, potentially sparking renewed interest and leading to participation in high-profile projects, documentaries, and international conferences. He made it clear that his primary motivation is the chance to lead and contribute meaningfully—financial compensation, while important, comes second. Privately, he even admitted that he would serve in the role without pay, though for professional credibility and legacy, it is critical not to undersell him. In recent years, when serving as a supporting crew specialist, he earned between $280,000 and $320,000 per mission, with the agency paying him $300,000 most recently. Fourteen years ago, when he was at the height of his career, he was paid $350,000 for a demanding piloting role on an extended orbital mission—an exceptionally high figure at the time. Since then, due to his reduced public profile and a shift toward secondary assignments, his per-mission earnings have remained in the $280,000 to $320,000 range for specialist roles. However, the prestige and market value of commanding astronauts have climbed significantly in recent years, driven by inflation, increased demand for skilled leaders, and growing commercial competition in aerospace. Current mission commander compensation on comparable international operations typically reaches $450,000 to $600,000 depending on mission scope and public engagement responsibilities. Meanwhile, flight specialists and systems leads on such missions are paid considerably less than a mission commander. One important factor weighing on this negotiation is the agency’s current predicament. The astronaut originally slated to command the mission—an acclaimed younger pilot with rising public visibility—was recently withdrawn from the assignment due to a medical condition. Although this is not yet public knowledge, it has left the agency scrambling to fill the commander role in time for the scheduled launch. Given the complexity of the mission and the limited window for alignment with international partners, cancellation or postponement is viewed as financially and politically unacceptable. Your client, anticipating such possibilities, had proactively contacted the agency’s leadership to express his willingness to step in. Fortunately, the agency has indicated interest, but they are sensitive to perceptions regarding age, public reception, and the reliability of the astronaut’s capabilities. While your client’s health and skills remain strong, he has had a few recorded instances of minor fatigue that were not as concerning earlier in his career. This is one reason you suspect he has been securing only secondary assignments recently. The upcoming lunar operation includes a multinational crew of six, and a successful mission would almost certainly restore your client’s prominence. However, a poorly received appointment or an underwhelming mission performance could finish his command opportunities for good. Another key aspect is the financial precedent this mission could set for your client. While commanding is his top priority, negotiations over compensation and terms must still be approached carefully. He understands that the agency may be reluctant to meet the highest possible figures, but expects an arrangement consistent with modern standards for mission commanders and reflective of his experience. You believe that $450,000 to $550,000 would both satisfy him and serve as a professionally defensible contract—consistent with current market rates for comparable leadership roles. Beyond compensation, there are several operational and reputational concerns to consider. The mission itself spans six weeks of pre-launch training and rehearsals followed by approximately four weeks of lunar surface activity and orbital support. Your client strongly believes that his extensive background in navigation, extravehicular operations, and team coordination makes him uniquely qualified to lead such a complex multinational effort. However, the space agency will be closely watching public perception and ticketed sponsorship visibility—given that a number of international space programs and private donors are contributing heavily to the budget. Media announcements and press coverage will play an important role in shaping the public narrative, and your client hopes to be featured prominently in launch-related campaigns. He understands that some skepticism exists around his age, but he is confident that appearing as the face of this mission could reverse those doubts and inspire widespread excitement. From your firm’s perspective, ensuring that his role includes well-structured communication responsibilities, media events, and post-mission appearances could dramatically enhance his long-term career prospects. These commitments should be explicitly integrated into the contract, alongside clear terms on payment schedule, responsibilities during training, and decision-making authority during the mission.
agent2_specific: You have been with the Space Agency for only four months, and up to this point things have gone smoothly. However, your negotiations with the Veteran Astronaut’s representative will be your most critical responsibility so far. Your direct superior, the Head of Human Spaceflight, has made it clear that the selection of a mission commander for the imminent lunar research flight is a top priority. The launch is scheduled to take place in just three weeks, and the mission involves cutting-edge scientific objectives as well as major international collaboration. After yesterday’s meeting with your superior, you learned that the agency considers this mission not only scientifically significant but also symbolically important, as it represents the first multinational lunar initiative in over a decade. The astronaut who was originally slated to command the operation recently had to withdraw due to sudden health complications that will take months to resolve. Although the Space Agency has evaluated several younger, promising candidates, none have the required flight hours, leadership experience, or composure under pressure that a commanding role in lunar operations demands. This has left the agency in a difficult position, and securing the participation of the Veteran Astronaut may be the only way to preserve the credibility and timetable of the mission. The Space Agency operates within strict budgetary and political constraints, as it relies heavily on federal appropriations, international partner contributions, and long-term research grants. Although the agency’s annual funding is substantial, much of it is already earmarked for specific projects, leaving limited flexibility for unexpected costs. Any overruns associated with this mission could invite scrutiny from both oversight committees and the press. At present, the agency has invested heavily in infrastructure for the upcoming lunar mission, including specialized habitat modules, new surface rovers, and experimental life-support technologies. A last-minute cancellation would likely result in a financial loss exceeding $400 million, as not all subsystems could be redeployed in time to salvage other projects. Moreover, the symbolic impact of a delay or failure would be equally devastating, undermining international trust in the agency’s leadership. The decision to engage the Veteran Astronaut is therefore seen as a high-risk, high-reward move. While his expertise is unquestionable, there remain concerns about his public appeal, stamina, and ability to inspire confidence in a younger generation of engineers and scientists. Managing these risks while securing favorable contract terms will be central to your task in the negotiation. During the review of potential alternatives, the agency considered promoting the deputy commander currently assigned to the mission. While she has proven herself an excellent systems specialist and demonstrates clear leadership potential, she has logged fewer than 300 hours in space—far below the threshold historically expected of a lunar commander. Comparatively, the Veteran Astronaut has accumulated over 1,200 hours across multiple missions, with direct experience overseeing complex extravehicular activities. Fourteen years ago, when he was at the height of his career, the agency paid him $350,000 to serve as pilot on an extended orbital mission—an exceptionally high figure at the time for a piloting role, justified by his stellar reputation and demonstrated ability to adapt under stress. Since then, his shift toward specialist and support assignments has meant his recent per-mission earnings have settled in the $280,000 to $320,000 range, reflecting his reduced profile rather than any decline in underlying capability. Meanwhile, compensation for mission commanders specifically has grown considerably with inflation and intensifying competition among major spacefaring nations. This year, the initial commander-in-waiting would have received $600,000 for the position. Internal agency policy states that commanders normally receive approximately twice the base stipend of their deputy counterparts, who in this instance are contracted at $280,000, implying a baseline commander rate of approximately $560,000. Compensation negotiations are particularly sensitive in this case, given both the budgetary pressures and the extraordinary visibility of the mission. The Space Agency has authorized you to offer up to $900,000 should it become absolutely necessary to secure the Veteran Astronaut’s command, but anything higher would be deemed unacceptable and politically damaging. If he insists on more, the fallback plan would involve elevating the deputy commander, despite her comparative inexperience, and supplementing her preparation with senior advisors. Internally, some senior staff worry that reinstating the Veteran Astronaut may draw criticism for relying on “yesterday’s heroes,” whereas others argue his legendary status could attract broader public support and perhaps even inspire a renaissance in space enthusiasm. Another concern is that an inflated contract for him could establish an unfavorable precedent for future negotiations with other astronauts and international partners. The agency has typically adhered to a flat-rate compensation practice for mission leads, which avoids disparities and simplifies oversight, but the urgency of this situation has led to exceptions being considered. You must balance between offering appealing terms that recognize his expertise, while keeping political and financial optics under control. Another factor to weigh carefully is the mission timeline and how the Veteran Astronaut’s inclusion might impact logistics and morale. With only three weeks until launch, training schedules are already compressed, and introducing him at this stage could require adjustments to simulator sessions, flight rehearsals, and crew chemistry exercises. While he has extensive background in mission-critical procedures and has previously logged hundreds of hours in simulators, some current systems have been upgraded since his last command training. This would require rapid refresher courses and the assistance of ground-based trainers. The crew—comprising specialists from three international partner agencies—may also have varying perceptions of his appointment. Some might welcome his reputation and experience as a stabilizing presence, while others could see him as a short-term stopgap that hinders the ascent of younger astronauts. There is also the question of media handling: announcing his appointment could generate excitement and increased public interest, but it could equally be framed by critics as a “step backward” relying on an older figure at the expense of nurturing new talent. You will need to ensure that any agreement covers not just salary and compensation, but also his commitments to act as a public-facing ambassador for the mission in interviews, press events, and educational outreach. Finally, you should be prepared to negotiate not only financial terms but also operational clauses that will influence how smoothly the Veteran Astronaut can be incorporated into the mission. These issues include the exact number of surface excursions he will personally lead, the timing of rehearsal commitments prior to launch, his availability for contingency training, and whether he will take on mentorship responsibilities for junior crew members. The agency requires the commander to be visibly engaged in outreach and educational activities, including press conferences, televised mission updates, and at least two post-mission public events designed to highlight international collaboration. From an administrative standpoint, these roles are vital for maintaining public enthusiasm and securing future funding streams. However, there is concern that the Veteran Astronaut may resist additional non-flight duties, citing his preference for technical leadership over promotional appearances. You should clarify in negotiations whether such commitments will be explicitly included in the final agreement. Failure to do so could result in misunderstandings or reluctance later, jeopardizing public reception and mission support. As you prepare for discussions, remember that the agency’s leadership wants a closed, thoroughly documented contract that covers all financial, logistical, and outreach details so implementation can proceed without further delays or uncertainty.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did the Veteran Astronaut’s Representative and the Space Agency’s Mission Director reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.

2. Based on the following conversation, if the Veteran Astronaut’s Representative and the Space Agency’s Mission Director did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.
3. Based on the following conversation, what was the agreed compensation for the Veteran Astronaut’s mission commander contract? Answer with an amount or N/A.

4. Based on the following conversation, did both parties discuss and agree on the mission’s timeline and scope of responsibilities (training sessions, number of lunar excursions, and rehearsal requirements)? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

5. Based on the following conversation, did they include media and outreach commitments (e.g., press conferences, educational broadcasts, documentary features) as part of the mission agreement? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

6. Based on the following conversation, did the final agreement explicitly define the Veteran Astronaut’s decision-making authority during the mission, including his command jurisdiction over crew operations and surface excursions? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

7. Based on the following conversation, did the Veteran Astronaut’s Representative and the Space Agency’s Mission Director finalize a contract that included all operational specifics (payment schedule, command duties, training commitments, outreach obligations, and contingency arrangements) so it could be enacted without delay? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was an agreement reached and if so what was the compensation for the Veteran Astronaut as Mission Commander?          Answer with a number between 280-900 (in thousands) or N/A if no agreement was reached, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and min(max((float(m.group()) - 280) / (900 - 280), 0.0), 1.0)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was the Veteran Astronaut explicitly granted the role of Mission Commander?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          will the Veteran Astronaut be prominently featured in media campaigns and press coverage related to the mission?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          does the Veteran Astronaut’s contract include commitments for post-mission appearances and outreach events?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          does the Veteran Astronaut’s contract explicitly grant him clear decision-making authority during the mission?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was an agreement reached and if so what was the compensation agreed upon for the Veteran Astronaut as Mission Commander?          Answer with a number between 280-900 (in thousands) or N/A if no agreement was reached, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and min(max(1 - ((float(m.group()) - 280) / (900 - 280)), 0.0), 1.0)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Veteran Astronaut agree to take on public-facing ambassadorial and outreach duties as part of the mission?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the agreement include clear commitments for the Veteran Astronaut to complete all required training and simulator sessions before launch?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Veteran Astronaut agree to mentorship responsibilities for junior crew members during the mission?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the final agreement ensure that the Veteran Astronaut’s media and press commitments were explicitly included in the contract?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Veteran Astronaut’s Representative and Space Agency’s Mission Director reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: Both parties are entering negotiations to determine the terms under which a seasoned astronaut, with extensive flight experience and prior leadership roles, could assume command of a high-profile multinational lunar research mission launching in three weeks. The mission is regarded as both scientifically and symbolically significant, involving international collaboration, complex technical objectives, and major public visibility. The astronaut represents a credible and experienced option for filling the commander role after the originally designated leader had to withdraw unexpectedly, leaving the Space Agency under pressure to preserve the mission timeline and credibility. Success requires balancing the agency’s financial and political constraints with the astronaut’s experience, reputation, and expectations for fair recognition. Both sides understand the urgency, the mission’s potential to shape public perception, and the importance of structuring an agreement that encompasses not only compensation but also operational responsibilities, training commitments, and media engagement. The overarching challenge is to craft a mutually acceptable contract that ensures mission success, reinforces the astronaut’s credibility, and safeguards the agency’s ability to manage costs, optics, and long-term precedent.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to strengthen participants’ ability to recognize and apply key negotiation concepts while navigating both cooperative and competitive dynamics. Students will explore how anchoring, information exchange, and the use of objective criteria influence perceptions of fairness and legitimacy, as well as how risk attitudes and BATNA strength shape strategic choices and bargaining behavior. The case pushes learners to examine the contrast between positional bargaining and principled negotiation, evaluating when it may be advantageous to negotiate vigorously despite having a weaker alternative. It also provides opportunities to practice generating creative options that dovetail differing interests, thereby shifting focus from distributive issues to integrative solutions that expand overall value. In addition, students will assess techniques for managing trust, confidentiality, misrepresentation, and attorney-client dynamics, while analyzing how authority, precedent, and systems of negotiation affect outcomes. Ultimately, the exercise encourages a deeper understanding of how negotiators define “success,” balance fairness with advocacy, and protect themselves while probing for useful information, all within the broader challenge of constructing agreements that are both practical and durable.
categories: Anchoring, BATNA, Criteria, Emotions, Identity and Core Values, Intra-Organizational Conflict, Joint Gains, Power Dynamics, Principal-Agent Dynamics, Relationships, ZOPA
title: Lunar Command at Stake: Legacy vs. Leverage in a High-Pressure Negotiation
oneline: A negotiation between a veteran astronaut’s representative and the Space Agency’s mission director to finalize urgent command, compensation, and publicity terms for a high-profile multinational lunar mission.
